danger_signals.visual_form: cars speeding across street at bottom of screen
danger_signals.meaning: environmental hazard; player must avoid or be shot
active threat; immediate danger of damage

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: cars visible at bottom screen boundary, sprite partially clipped in some versions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies continuously patrol and fire; player must navigate around moving hazards

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: sprite rendering differs between North American version (clipped off-screen) and European version (full sprite visible against dark background)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: same enemy behavior; visual presentation affected by technical constraints

environmental_storytelling.example: cars at bottom of screen establish street-level danger zone; suggests player is on urban Mars colony street under Recall Corporation surveillance
partial sprite visibility in NA version creates unintended visual effect; European version shows fuller hostile vehicle design against dark background, improving readability of threat

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: speed across screen in continuous motion; fire bullets at player; represent automated or controlled security presence

notes: Total Recall NES is noted as a poor adaptation of the 1990 film. Available source material focuses on development/production issues (unused movie theme music, missing composer tracks) rather than in-depth world-state communication mechanics. Visual communication of danger and faction identity inferred from documented sprite and hazard placement only. Game likely uses arcade-style hazard avoidance rather than deep narrative world-state signaling given platformer paradigm and era. Tier T3 data reflects limited documentary evidence.
